narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 95% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure means the model flagged your application as too uncertain for admission, prioritizing students with stronger signals of bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this prediction was your ranking in Year 3 grades, where you placed in the bottom decile of your school—far below the middle-to-upper deciles typical for students who go on to pass the bar. This low Year 3 performance stood out as the strongest signal to the model, suggesting potential challenges in sustaining the academic rigor needed for law school and the bar. Next in influence was your family's middle income bracket, which, while common overall, appears less frequently among bar passers compared to those from upper-middle or high-income backgrounds, nudging the prediction further toward risk. Your Year 1 grade decile in the second lowest group also contributed in the same direction, though less strongly, as it was again well below the averages for successful students. In contrast, the model noted some neutral or typical aspects, like your choice of full-time study, which aligns with most bar passers, but these couldn't offset the top concerns. Compared to admitted students, who typically have LSAT scores around 37 and undergraduate GPAs near 3.24, your scores of 31.5 and 2.6 were lower, reinforcing the overall picture, though your later-year deciles carried the heaviest weight. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as an Asian woman pursuing full-time law studies from a middle-income background—these paths have succeeded for many, but the model weighs early and mid-undergrad struggles heavily here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from bar-passed alumni to build a stronger narrative for your next application. Reapplying after addressing these core academic signals could shift the model's view in your favor, as even modest improvements in decile rankings have turned predictions around for similar profiles. Keep pushing—you have the determination, and targeted steps can change this outcome.
